content.text: Certain primes have special forms that have attracted particular attention.

content.title: Mersenne Number
content.content:
A **Mersenne number** is an integer of the form 
$M_n = 2^n - 1$.

A Mersenne number that is prime is called a **Mersenne prime**.

content.title: Condition for Mersenne Primes
content.content: If $M_n = 2^n - 1$ is prime, then $n$ is prime.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
We prove the contrapositive. If $n = ab$ with $1 < a, b < n$, then
\[
2^n - 1 = 2^{ab} - 1 = (2^a)^b - 1 = (2^a - 1)((2^a)^{b-1} + (2^a)^{b-2} + \cdots + 1).
\]
Since $1 < 2^a - 1 < 2^n - 1$, $M_n$ is composite.

content.title: Known Mersenne Primes
content.problem:
The first several Mersenne primes are:
\[
M_2 = 3, \quad M_3 = 7, \quad M_5 = 31, \quad M_7 = 127, \quad M_{13} = 8191.
\]
As of 2024, only 51 Mersenne primes are known. The largest known prime is 
typically a Mersenne prime.

content.title: Fermat Number
content.content:
A **Fermat number** is an integer of the form 
$F_n = 2^{2^n} + 1$.

content.title: Condition for Fermat Numbers
content.content: If $2^m + 1$ is prime for $m \geq 1$, then $m$ is a power of 2.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
If $m$ has an odd divisor $d > 1$, write $m = dk$. Then $2^m + 1 = (2^k)^d + 1$ 
is divisible by $2^k + 1$ (since $x^d + 1$ is divisible by $x + 1$ when 
$d$ is odd). Thus $2^m + 1$ is composite.

content.title: Fermat Numbers
content.problem:
$$
F_0 &= 3 & F_1 &= 5 & F_2 &= 17 \\
F_3 &= 257 & F_4 &= 65537 & F_5 &= 4294967297
$$
Fermat conjectured all $F_n$ are prime. However, Euler showed $F_5 = 641 \times 6700417$. 
No Fermat prime beyond $F_4$ has been found.

content.text:
Fermat primes are connected to constructible regular polygons: a regular 
$n$-gon is constructible with compass and straightedge if and only if $n$ 
is a power of 2 times a product of distinct Fermat primes.

content.title: Dirichlet's Theorem
content.content:
If $a$ and $d$ are relatively prime positive integers, then the arithmetic 
progression
\[
a, \, a + d, \, a + 2d, \, a + 3d, \, \ldots
\]
contains infinitely many primes.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
This deep theorem, proved by Dirichlet in 1837, requires techniques from 
analytic number theory.

content.title: Dirichlet's Theorem
content.problem:
The progression $3, 7, 11, 15, 19, 23, \ldots$ (where $a = 3$, $d = 4$) 
contains infinitely many primes. These are exactly the primes of the form 
$4k + 3$.
